invariant_definition: Without qualities. *Nirguṇa brahman* is *brahman* in its highest description, beyond all qualifying attributes. This is the strict Advaita usage; non-Advaita schools restrict the scope of *nirguṇa* (Viśiṣṭādvaita: it denies *heya-guṇas* only; Dvaita: it denies *prākṛta-guṇas* only). The user's position-brief connects *nirguṇa brahman* to Aurobindo's static-aspect Saccidānanda and to Deleuze's univocal being — see T1 of the brief.

per_school.definition: *Nirguṇa brahman* is the highest description of *brahman* — partless, attribute-less, beyond determination. The *neti neti* (not-this, not-this) of Bṛhadāraṇyaka 2.3.6 expresses this. *Saguṇa brahman* is sublatable in *nirguṇa brahman*-realization.
*Nirguṇa* in *śruti* denies *heya-guṇas* (defects), not all qualities. Brahman is *nir-guṇa* in the sense of *nis-prākṛta-guṇa* (free of inert-matter-qualities) and *nir-doṣa* (free of defects).
*Nirguṇa* denies only *prākṛta-guṇas*; Viṣṇu has infinite real auspicious qualities.

translator_note: 'Without qualities' or 'attributeless' acceptable. The Advaita *nirguṇa brahman* is structurally close to the *Deus absconditus* / negative-theology tradition (Eckhart, the *Cloud of Unknowing*) but with the difference that *nirguṇa* is what is realized in liberation, not merely posited beyond cognition.
